intro: Transitioning code from C# to Rust shifts an application from a managed runtime environment (.NET) to a system-level language that eliminates garbage collection entirely through compile-time ownership tracking. Developers trade object-oriented hierarchies and runtime abstraction layers for absolute memory safety, predictable performance, and bare-metal execution control. This change guarantees zero undefined behavior and optimal memory usage without a heavy runtime footprint.

diffs.from: Static, nominal object system featuring runtime type metadata reflection, reified generics, and class inheritance.
diffs.to: Static, structural and nominal type layout featuring zero-cost generics, traits, and powerful algebraic data types (enums).

diffs.from: Imperative brace-scoped design, property shortcuts, automated class definitions, and integrated LINQ syntax engines.
diffs.to: Expression-based layout utilizing strict block rules, explicit macro invocations, pattern matching, and minimal boilerplate.

diffs.from: Managed via a multi-generational runtime garbage collector tracking object lifetimes dynamically on the heap.
diffs.to: Compile-time ownership semantics tracking resource lifecycles via strict single-owner borrowing and scope lifetime validation rules.

diffs.from: Task Parallel Library running async operations across system thread pools using standard async/await keywords.
diffs.to: Zero-cost asynchronous futures architecture requiring an explicit runtime engine execution host (like Tokyo or async-std).

diffs.from: Comprehensive corporate runtime providing web servers, database connectors, and enterprise infrastructure components.
diffs.to: Minimal systems foundation focusing strictly on basic safety primitives, OS abstractions, and high-performance containers.

examples.title: Pattern Matching Option Handling
examples.description: Compares C# null-checking validations against a type-safe Rust algebraic Option enum pattern matching block.

examples.title: Thread-Safe Data Composition
examples.description: Demonstrates converting a C# thread-safe class property lock setup into a modern Rust mutually isolated Arc/Mutex wrapper.

tips.title: Internalize Ownership and Borrowing
tips.body: Understand Rust's core constraint: data has exactly one owner at a time. To pass variables without transferring ownership, learn to use references (`&T` for shared read-only, `&mut T` for exclusive write permissions).

tips.title: Replace Inheritance with Traits
tips.body: Rust completely rejects object-oriented class inheritance structures. Translate C# abstract base classes and interfaces into highly performant, static Rust Traits to achieve decoupling.

tips.title: Pick an Asynchronous Executor
tips.body: Rust has async/await built into the language syntax, but lacks a built-in async runtime engine. Add the `tokio` crate dependency to your project to replicate the asynchronous thread scheduling behavior of C#'s TPL.

tips.title: Use Result Over Exceptions
tips.body: Rust does not throw exceptions. Functions that can fail must return a `Result<T, E>` enum. Use the question mark operator (`?`) to propagate errors cleanly up the call stack without structural `try/catch` clutter.
